(or, unofficially or obsolete, **městečko** [literally "small town"]), translated as "[market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town")", is a status conferred on certain [municipalities](/wiki/Obec "Obec") in the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), lying in terms of size and importance higher than that of simple *[obec](/wiki/Obec "Obec")* (municipality) but lower than that of *město* ([city](/wiki/City "City"), [town](/wiki/Town "Town")).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Status conferred on certain municipalities in the Czech Republic

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article includes a [list of references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources"), [related reading](/wiki/Wikipedia:Further_reading "Wikipedia:Further reading"), or [external links](/wiki/Wikipedia:External_links "Wikipedia:External links"), **but its sources remain unclear because it lacks [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")**. Please help [improve](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Fact_and_Reference_Check "Wikipedia:WikiProject Fact and Reference Check") this article by [introducing](/wiki/Wikipedia:When_to_cite "Wikipedia:When to cite") more precise citations. *(February 2017)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

**Městys** (or, unofficially or obsolete, **městečko** [literally "small town"]), translated as "[market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town")", is a status conferred on certain [municipalities](/wiki/Obec "Obec") in the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), lying in terms of size and importance higher than that of simple *[obec](/wiki/Obec "Obec")* (municipality) but lower than that of *město* ([city](/wiki/City "City"), [town](/wiki/Town "Town")).

Historically, a *městys* was a locality that had the right to stage [livestock](/wiki/Livestock "Livestock") markets (and some other "extraordinary" and annual markets), and it is therefore translated as "market town". The term went out of official use in [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") in 1954 but was reintroduced in the Czech Republic in 2006. As of September 2020, there are 228 municipalities on which the status of *městys* has been re-admitted. In all cases, these are municipalities that have requested the return of their former title. This title has not been newly awarded to any municipality that would not have it in the past—the law does not even set any specific criteria for it, only procedural competencies are established. There are no special rights or competences under this title other than the right to use this word together with the name of the municipality and its bodies.

## Historical development of *městys* in the Czech Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%9Bstys&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Historical development of městys in the Czech Republic")]

### Middle Ages and early modern period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%9Bstys&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Middle Ages and early modern period")]

The development of the *městečko* began in the 14th century, decreed by the reigning monarch primarily on the land of the nobility and church. From the 16th century on, it became possible for settlements with significant springs to get the title *městys*. The village then became entitled to call itself "městec", resulting in such place names as [Městec Králové](/wiki/M%C4%9Bstec_Kr%C3%A1lov%C3%A9 "Městec Králové") and [Heřmanův Městec](/wiki/He%C5%99man%C5%AFv_M%C4%9Bstec "Heřmanův Městec").

The typical *městečko* was very small, and a large number were created during the 14th century. Eduard Maur estimated that the average distance between two *městečka* was 5–20 km.

### Early 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%9Bstys&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early 20th century")]

After the creation of [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") in 1918, the status was conferred by the Ministerial Council on the basis of a detailed application from the village. According to the "*Statistický lexikon obcí v Republice československé*" (Statistical lexicon of villages in Czechoslovakia), there were 503 *městys* in Czechoslovakia in 1930.

After the [coup d'état of February 1948](/wiki/Czech_coup "Czech coup"), the Communist ruling authorities ceased using the term by around 1950.

### Late 20th century revival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%9Bstys&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Late 20th century revival")]

After the [Velvet Revolution](/wiki/Velvet_Revolution "Velvet Revolution") of 1989, there was intense lobbying for the revival of the *městys* status from the representatives of villages which had previously held the title. On 27 January 2006 their efforts succeeded; the [Chamber of Deputies of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_the_Czech_Republic "Chamber of Deputies of the Czech Republic") approved an amendment to the Municipalities Act (Establishment of Municipalities) [[1]](http://www.psp.cz/sqw/historie.sqw?o=4&T=987), which amongst other matters restored the title of *městys*. The amendment was approved by the [Senate of the Czech Republic](/wiki/Senate_of_the_Czech_Republic "Senate of the Czech Republic") on 29 March, was signed by the president on 11 May and came into force on 1 July 2006.

Municipalities that had the right to carry the title of *městys* prior to 17 May 1954 are to inform the chairman of the Chamber of Deputies, so that the restoration of the status can be arranged.

On 10 October 2006, the chairman of the Chamber of Deputies announced the first 108 municipalities which can continue to use the title.

## Similar designations in other countries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%9Bstys&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Similar designations in other countries")]

Many other countries in Europe have similar designations, for example:

* Hungary and Slovakia: the Latin title *oppidum*; from 14th century to 1871 also [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language"): *mezőváros* - lit. "field town"
* Austria: [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Marktgemeinde*. In other [German-speaking areas](/wiki/German-speaking_Europe "German-speaking Europe") the terms *Flecken*, *Marktflecken* and *Markt* are also used.
* Denmark: [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language"): *flække*
* Sweden: [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"): *köping*
* France: [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *bourg*

*Městyse* also existed on the territory of the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), and before Communism these municipalities often used the terms:

* [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *[Miasteczko](/wiki/Miasteczko "Miasteczko")*
* [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): местечко (*mestechko*)
* [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanian_language "Lithuanian language"): *miestelis*
* [Latvian](/wiki/Latvian_language "Latvian language"): *miests*
* [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): містечко (*mistechko*)
* [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language"): שטעטל (*[shtetl](/wiki/Shtetl "Shtetl")*)
